sections.h:
Western Australia Police Force

SPECIAL OPERATIONS APPLICATION UNIT

SURVEILLANCE DEVICES ACT 2004 (Cth)

PROCEDURES
sections.b: Western Australia Police Force SPECIAL OPERATIONS APPLICATION UNIT SURVEILLANCE DEVICES ACT 2004 (Cth) PROCEDURES

sections.t: Making Applications for Surveillance Device Warrants
sections.h:
The provisions of the Act allow the use of surveillance devices as a tool to assist the investigation of a relevant offence. Applications for warrants, emergency authorisations and tracking device authorisations under the provisions of the Act must comply with the legislation. The provisions of the Act regulate:

four different types of surveillance devices, including a data surveillance device, a listening device, an optical surveillance device and a tracking device;

types of warrant, including a surveillance device warrant, retrieval warrant and computer access warrant; and three different locations whereby surveillance devices, subject to a warrant, can be installed, including in a specified premises, a specified object and with respect to a specified person.

An application for a surveillance device warrant must:

state the name of the applicant;

state the nature and duration of the warrant sought, including the kind of surveillance device or devices sought to be authorised; and be supported by an affidavit setting out the grounds on which the warrant is sought.

An application for a surveillance device warrant can be made either in writing or orally (usually by telephone). The nature and circumstances that require the use of the surveillance device will determine how the application is made. All application for surveillance device warrants will proceed in the following manner:

the investigating officer will liaise with the Intelligence and Communications Police Technical Unit to determine the capability of surveillance device resources. Should suitable surveillance device resources be available the investigating officer will be notified of the requirement to obtain a surveillance device warrant – in certain circumstances surveillance devices can be utilised without a warrant;

a completed and approved Tasking Request Form (available from the Western Australia Police Force Intranet) will be lodged through the investigating officer’s Divisional/District Office to the Intelligence and Communications Covert Coordinator;

the investigating officer must include in the Tasking Request Form the following information:

an outline of the investigation, including the offences being investigated;

the details of any premises, object or person with whom the surveillance devices will be deployed against;

information outlining how the use of surveillance devices forms part of the investigative strategy; and any other matter that may be of relevance to the Intelligence and Communications Covert Coordinator in determining the approval of the request.

sections.h:
Upon advice from the Intelligence and Communications Covert Coordinator to proceed, the investigating officer will provide a draft affidavit in the approved form (Special Operations Application Unit can be contacted for a draft affidavit template), by electronic means to Special Operations Application Unit containing the facts and other grounds on which the application for the surveillance device warrant is based. The draft affidavit will be prepared by the investigating officer or by an officer who is immediately involved in the investigation and who is familiar with the facts and events in the investigation. The content of the affidavit is to address three principal matters, these being:

one or more relevant offences have been, are being, are about to be, or are likely to be, committed;

an investigation into those offences is being, will be, or is likely to be, conducted; and the use of a surveillance device is necessary in the course of that investigation for the purpose of enabling evidence to be obtained of the commission of the relevant offences or the identity or location of the offenders.

The Act does not limit the use of surveillance devices in respect of a particular offence, however, when considering an application for a surveillance device warrant the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member must have regard to the following matters which must be addressed in the affidavit:

the nature and gravity of the alleged offence, or if in relation to the safe recovery of a child to whom a recovery order relates, the circumstances that gave rise to the making of the order;

the extent to which the privacy of any person is likely to be affected;

the existence of any alternative means of obtaining the evidence or information sought to be obtained;

the likely evidentiary or intelligence value of any evidence or information sought to be obtained; and any previous warrant sought or issued under the Act in connection with the same alleged offence or the same recovery order.

Where possible, the affidavit will be sworn by the member in charge of the investigation, however any other officer of Western Australia Police Force may make an application on that person’s behalf. Special Operations Application Unit will be responsible for making applications on behalf of regional Western Australia locations. The draft affidavit should be developed in consultation with and submitted to Special Operations Application Unit. Special Operations Application Unit will:

conduct a thorough research of all information relevant to the investigation to ensure that the affidavit is complete. Information from the following sources will be added by Special Operations Application Unit to the affidavit, including (where applicable):

researching Information Data Management system;

researching Incident Management System;

reviewing the case file;

reviewing witness statements;

reviewing post mortem results;

reviewing drug analysis reports;

reviewing information provided by informants;

reviewing AUSTRAC reports;

reviewing telecommunications interception product;

reviewing surveillance devices product;

reviewing surveillance running sheets;

reviewing Undercover Police Unit running sheets;

reviewing Australian Crime Commission, Australian Federal Police and Australian Customs Service intelligence holdings (should it be required); and reviewing and researching any other information deemed relevant.

thoroughly examine the affidavit to ensure it is presented in the correct format, paying particular attention to style, presentation and font;

thoroughly examine the affidavit to ensure it complies with legislative requirements, including oversight requirements by an inspection entity;

proof read the affidavit to ensure the content is clear, concise and the information flows from one paragraph to the next;

ensure the affidavit is of the highest standard and is consistent with previous affidavits to provide the best opportunity to be approved by an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member;

enter the details of surveillance device applications onto Special Operations Application Unit databases and allocate a warrant designator;

draft an application and warrant form; and

collate all the relevant documents and any additional information and submit to a Western Australia Police Force solicitor.

The solicitor is responsible for checking the entire application to ensure that it complies with the legislation and that the documentation is of an appropriate standard to be submitted to an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member. When complete and ready for signature, the investigating officer will check the application for accuracy and content and sign the affidavit before a Justice of the Peace.

Special Operations Application Unit will collate the documents required to proceed to Court. These will consist of:

the affidavit, sworn by the member in charge of the investigation;

the application cover sheet, appropriately endorsed with the relevant information and signed by the applicant;

the draft warrant form, appropriately endorsed with the relevant information; and an expiry date indicator.

PLEASE NOTE: Commonwealth Inspection findings: Affidavits should include specific details about the privacy impacts on any person, and the existence of alternative means (and/or more traditional forms of surveillance) of obtaining evidence. When using particularly intrusive technologies, WA Police must provide sufficient details in its affidavits to enable the issuing authority to have regard to the use, and capability of the technology and the extent to which the privacy of any person is likely to be affected.

sections.t: Attending the Family Court of Australia or Administrative Appeals Tribunal
sections.h:
Special Operations Application Unit will contact the court officer at the Family Court of Australia or the Administrative Appeals Tribunal and advise the court officer that Western Australia Police Force wish to proceed with the matter and request that an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member be made available to consider the warrant. The court officer will advise of the time of the hearing and those persons the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member requires to attend. Protocol requires that the applicant attend accompanied by a solicitor acting on behalf of Western Australia Police Force, however it is the eligible Judge’s or nominated AAT member’s prerogative whether the protocol is adhered to and may choose that only the applicant or Solicitor appear, or neither.

A Special Operations Application Unit officer will attend the Family Court of Australia or the AAT with the relevant documents and those persons required by the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member. Upon the warrant being signed, the solicitor will inspect the warrant paying particular attention to:

the expiry date, which should match the expiry date indicator for the number of days requested – if the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member has chosen another date, it should match the number of days on the warrant, which should not be more than 90 days; and any restrictions or conditions.

sections.h:
Once the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member has issued the surveillance device warrant all documentation will be conveyed by the Special Operations Application Unit officer to the Warrant Administration section of Special Operations Application Unit for the purposes of recording and compliance. A copy of the warrant will be provided to the Police Technical Unit by Special Operations Application Unit.

Should the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member refuse to grant the warrant, this information must be reported on the General Register, along with the reason for the refusal. The Special Operations Application Unit officer will advise the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit, who will liaise with the investigating officer and determine whether or not to make an amended application in relation to the warrant sought. All application documents must be kept.

sections.t: Making Applications for Extensions/variations to Surveillance Device Warrants
sections.h:
The Act provides for an officer of Western Australia Police Force to apply for an extension to the period the warrant is in force, up to a maximum of 90 days after the day the warrant would have otherwise expired, or for a variation of any of the other terms of the warrant. A completed and approved Supplementary Tasking Request Form, available from the Western Australia Police Force Intranet, is required to be considered by the Intelligence and Communications Covert Coordinator. The substantial requirements of the original application for a surveillance device warrant will also apply to the application for an extension or variation to an existing warrant, including the requirement for an affidavit outlining all the original information contained in the original affidavit, accompanied by the new information justifying the need to extend or vary the particular warrant.

Where possible, the affidavit will be sworn by the member in charge of the investigation, however any other officer of Western Australia Police Force may make an application on that person’s behalf. Special Operations Application Unit will be responsible for making applications on behalf of regional Western Australia locations. The draft affidavit should be developed in consultation with and submitted to Special Operations Application Unit. Special Operations Application Unit will:

conduct a thorough research of all information relevant to the investigation to ensure that the affidavit is complete. Information from the following sources will be added by Special Operations Application Unit to the affidavit, including (where applicable):

researching Information Data Management system;

researching Incident Management System;

reviewing the case file;

reviewing witness statements;

reviewing post mortem results;

reviewing drug analysis reports;

reviewing information provided by informants;

reviewing AUSTRAC reports;

reviewing telecommunications interception product;

reviewing surveillance devices product;

reviewing surveillance running sheets;

reviewing Undercover Police Unit running sheets;

reviewing Australian Crime Commission, Australian Federal Police and Australian Customs Service intelligence holdings (should it be required); and reviewing and researching any other information deemed relevant.

thoroughly examine the affidavit to ensure it is presented in the correct format, paying particular attention to style, presentation and font;

thoroughly examine the affidavit to ensure it complies with legislative requirements, including oversight requirements by an inspection entity;

proof read the affidavit to ensure the content is clear, concise and the information flows from one paragraph to the next;

ensure the affidavit is of the highest standard and is consistent with previous affidavits to provide the best opportunity to be approved by an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member;

enter the details of surveillance device applications onto Special Operations Application Unit databases and allocate a warrant designator;

draft an application and warrant form; and

collate all the relevant documents and any additional information and submit to a Western Australia Police Force solicitor.

The solicitor is responsible for checking the entire application to ensure that it complies with the legislation and that the documentation is of an appropriate standard to be submitted to an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member. When complete and ready for signature, the investigating officer will check the application for accuracy and content and sign the affidavit before a Justice of the Peace.

Special Operations Application Unit will collate the documents required to proceed to Court. These will consist of:

the affidavit, sworn by the member in charge of the investigation;

the application cover sheet, appropriately endorsed with the relevant information and signed by the applicant;

the draft warrant form, appropriately endorsed with the relevant information; and an expiry date indicator.

The Special Operations Application Unit officer will then arrange for the warrant application to be heard by an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member as per the process outlined at ATTENDING THE FAMILY COURT OF AUSTRALIA OR ADMINISTRATIVE APPEALS TRIBUNAL.

MAKING APPLICATIONS FOR EMERGENCY AUTHORISATIONS AND SUBSEQUENT APPLICATION TO AN ELIGIBLE JUDGE OR A NOMINATED AAT MEMBER

Under certain urgent circumstances, an appropriate authorising officer may consider applications for an emergency authorisation for the use of a surveillance device which would otherwise require a warrant. Before making an application to an appropriate authorising officer for an emergency authorisation, the applicant must reasonably suspect that:

in relation to an investigation of a relevant offence:

an imminent risk of serious violence to a person or substantial damage to property exists;

the use of a surveillance device is immediately necessary for the purpose of dealing with that risk;

the circumstances are so serious and the matter is of such urgency that the use of a surveillance device is warranted; and it is not practicable in the circumstances to apply for a surveillance device warrant; or in relation to a recovery order that is in force:

the circumstances are so urgent as to warrant the immediate use of a surveillance device; and it is not practicable in the circumstances to apply for a surveillance device warrant.

The application may be made orally, in writing or by telephone, fax, email or any other means of communication. The appropriate authorising officer may give the emergency authorisation if satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for the suspicion founding the application.

Should an appropriate authorising officer give an emergency authorisation, that officer must, as soon as practicable, make a written record of the giving of that authorisation, including in the record:

the name of the applicant for the authorisation;

the date and time the authorisation was given; and

the nature of the authorisation given.

This original written record made by the appropriate authorising officer is to be provided to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit, who will cause the record to be filed with the appropriate documentation and ensure the details of the emergency authorisation are entered into the General Register.

Within 48 hours after giving an emergency authorisation the appropriate authorising officer who gave the authorisation, or another person on the appropriate authorising officer’s behalf, must apply to an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member for approval of the giving of the emergency authorisation. It is possible during the same application to apply for a warrant authorising the ongoing use of the surveillance device, providing all the normal legislative requirements are met as per a standard warrant application under the Act. The application:

must specify:

the name of the applicant for the approval; and

the kind or kinds of surveillance device to which the emergency authorisation relates and, if a warrant is sought, the nature and duration of the warrant;

must be supported by an affidavit setting out the grounds on which the approval (and warrant, if any) is sought; and must be accompanied by a copy of the written record made after the giving of the emergency authorisation.

A copy of this application must be kept in the relevant Codafile by the responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer.

Special Operations Application Unit will assist the appropriate authorising officer, or the officer acting on the appropriate authorising officer’s behalf, in relation to this application, in a manner substantially similar to the process followed in the normal application for a surveillance device warrant. The draft affidavit should be developed in consultation with and submitted to Special Operations Application Unit. Special Operations Application Unit will:

conduct a thorough research of all information relevant to the investigation to ensure that the affidavit is complete. Information from the following sources will be added by Special Operations Application Unit to the affidavit, including (where applicable):

researching Information Data Management system;

researching Incident Management System;

reviewing the case file;

Special Operations Application Unit will collate the documents required to proceed to Court. These will consist of: the affidavit, sworn by the member in charge of the investigation; the application cover sheet, appropriately endorsed with the relevant information and signed by the applicant; the draft warrant form, appropriately endorsed with the relevant information; and an expiry date indicator. The Special Operations Application Unit officer will then arrange for the warrant application to be heard by an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member as per the process outlined at ATTENDING THE FAMILY COURT OF AUSTRALIA OR ADMINISTRATIVE APPEALS TRIBUNAL. MAKING APPLICATIONS FOR EMERGENCY AUTHORISATIONS AND SUBSEQUENT APPLICATION TO AN ELIGIBLE JUDGE OR A NOMINATED AAT MEMBER Under certain urgent circumstances, an appropriate authorising officer may consider applications for an emergency authorisation for the use of a surveillance device which would otherwise require a warrant. Before making an application to an appropriate authorising officer for an emergency authorisation, the applicant must reasonably suspect that: in relation to an investigation of a relevant offence: an imminent risk of serious violence to a person or substantial damage to property exists; the use of a surveillance device is immediately necessary for the purpose of dealing with that risk; the circumstances are so serious and the matter is of such urgency that the use of a surveillance device is warranted; and it is not practicable in the circumstances to apply for a surveillance device warrant; or in relation to a recovery order that is in force: the circumstances are so urgent as to warrant the immediate use of a surveillance device; and it is not practicable in the circumstances to apply for a surveillance device warrant. The application may be made orally, in writing or by telephone, fax, email or any other means of communication. The appropriate authorising officer may give the emergency authorisation if satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for the suspicion founding the application. Should an appropriate authorising officer give an emergency authorisation, that officer must, as soon as practicable, make a written record of the giving of that authorisation, including in the record: the name of the applicant for the authorisation; the date and time the authorisation was given; and the nature of the authorisation given. This original written record made by the appropriate authorising officer is to be provided to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit, who will cause the record to be filed with the appropriate documentation and ensure the details of the emergency authorisation are entered into the General Register. Within 48 hours after giving an emergency authorisation the appropriate authorising officer who gave the authorisation, or another person on the appropriate authorising officer’s behalf, must apply to an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member for approval of the giving of the emergency authorisation. It is possible during the same application to apply for a warrant authorising the ongoing use of the surveillance device, providing all the normal legislative requirements are met as per a standard warrant application under the Act. The application: must specify: the name of the applicant for the approval; and the kind or kinds of surveillance device to which the emergency authorisation relates and, if a warrant is sought, the nature and duration of the warrant; must be supported by an affidavit setting out the grounds on which the approval (and warrant, if any) is sought; and must be accompanied by a copy of the written record made after the giving of the emergency authorisation. A copy of this application must be kept in the relevant Codafile by the responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer. Special Operations Application Unit will assist the appropriate authorising officer, or the officer acting on the appropriate authorising officer’s behalf, in relation to this application, in a manner substantially similar to the process followed in the normal application for a surveillance device warrant. The draft affidavit should be developed in consultation with and submitted to Special Operations Application Unit. Special Operations Application Unit will: conduct a thorough research of all information relevant to the investigation to ensure that the affidavit is complete. Information from the following sources will be added by Special Operations Application Unit to the affidavit, including (where applicable): researching Information Data Management system; researching Incident Management System; reviewing the case file; reviewing witness statements; reviewing post mortem results; reviewing drug analysis reports; reviewing information provided by informants; reviewing AUSTRAC reports; reviewing telecommunications interception product; reviewing surveillance devices product; reviewing surveillance running sheets; reviewing Undercover Police Unit running sheets; reviewing Australian Crime Commission, Australian Federal Police and Australian Customs Service intelligence holdings (should it be required); and reviewing and researching any other information deemed relevant. thoroughly examine the affidavit to ensure it is presented in the correct format, paying particular attention to style, presentation and font; thoroughly examine the affidavit to ensure it complies with legislative requirements, including oversight requirements by an inspection entity; proof read the affidavit to ensure the content is clear, concise and the information flows from one paragraph to the next; ensure the affidavit is of the highest standard and is consistent with previous affidavits to provide the best opportunity to be approved by an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member; enter the details of surveillance device applications onto Special Operations Application Unit databases and allocate a warrant designator; draft an application and warrant form; and collate all the relevant documents and any additional information and submit to a Western Australia Police Force solicitor. The solicitor is responsible for checking the entire application to ensure that it complies with the legislation and that the documentation is of an appropriate standard to be submitted to an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member. When complete and ready for signature, the appropriate authorising officer, or the officer acting on the appropriate authorising officer’s behalf, will check the application for accuracy and content and sign the affidavit before a Justice of the Peace. Special Operations Application Unit will collate the documents required to proceed to Court. These will consist of: the affidavit, sworn by the appropriate authorising officer or the officer acting on the appropriate authorising officer’s behalf; the application cover sheet, appropriately endorsed with the relevant information and signed by the applicant; the draft warrant form, appropriately endorsed with the relevant information; and an expiry date indicator. The Special Operations Application Unit officer will then arrange for the application to be heard by an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member as per the process outlined at ATTENDING THE FAMILY COURT OF AUSTRALIA OR ADMINISTRATIVE APPEALS TRIBUNAL.

sections.t: Making Applications for Tracking Device Authorisations and Retrieval of Tracking Devices
sections.h:
Under certain circumstances, an appropriate authorising officer may give a tracking device authorisation to an officer of Western Australia Police Force, authorising the use of a tracking device without a warrant in the investigation of a relevant offence, or in the location and safe recovery of a child to whom a recovery order relates. The appropriate authorising officer may also authorise the retrieval, without a warrant, of a tracking device to which a tracking device authorisation relates.

An appropriate authorising officer must not give permission under the Act for the use, installation or retrieval of a tracking device if the installation of the device, or its retrieval, involves entry onto premises without permission or an interference with the interior of a vehicle without permission.

The applicant, being an officer of Western Australia Police Force, must apply orally or in writing to the appropriate authorising officer and must address in that application the matters that would be required to be addressed as if the officer were making an application for a surveillance device warrant or a retrieval warrant, as the case requires.

Should an appropriate authorising officer give a tracking device authorisation, that officer must, as soon as practicable, make a written record of the giving of that authorisation, including in the record:

the name of the applicant for the authorisation;

the date and time the authorisation was given; and

the nature of the authorisation given, including where applicable:

the alleged offence or offences in respect of which the authorisation is given;

if in relation to a recovery order, the date the order was made and the name of the child to whom the order relates;

the object or class of object in or on which the use of the tracking device is authorised;

the vehicle or class of vehicle on which the use of the tracking device is authorised;

the name of the specified person of which the use of the tracking device is authorised; and the premises or object from which the tracking device is to be retrieved, if authorising the retrieval of a tracking device;

the name of the officer of Western Australian Police who is primarily responsible for executing the authorisation; and any conditions subject to which a tracking device may be used, under the authorisation.

This original written record made by the appropriate authorising officer is to be provided to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit, who will cause the record to be filed with the appropriate documentation and ensure the details of the tracking device authorisation are entered into the General Register.

COMPUTER ACCESS WARRANTS:

Computer access warrants, which allows law enforcement:

to covertly access devices to investigate serious crimes to search devices such as laptops, mobile phones and USBs, and collect information to conceal the fact that a device has been accessed

Law enforcement computer access warrants must be issued by an independent authority (a judge or AAT member) and cannot authorise interference with, or material loss or damage to, a computer.

Computer access warrants can be sought only for serious offences (offences that attract a penalty of three years or more).

sections.t: Making Applications for A Computer Access Warrant
sections.h:
Warrants sought for offence investigations

(1)  A law enforcement officer (or another person on the law enforcement officer's behalf) may apply for the issue of a computer access warrant if the law enforcement officer suspects on reasonable grounds that:

(a)  one or more relevant offences have been, are being, are about to be, or are likely to be, committed; and

(b)  an investigation into those offences is being, will be, or is likely to be, conducted; and

(c)  access to data held in a computer (the target computer ) is necessary, in the course of that investigation, for the purpose of enabling evidence to be obtained of:                               (i)  the commission of those offences; or                              (ii)  the identity or location of the offenders.              (2)  If the application is being made by or on behalf of a State or Territory law enforcement officer, the reference in subsection (1) to a relevant offence does not include a reference to a State offence that has a federal aspect.

Warrants sought for recovery orders

(3)  A law enforcement officer (or another person on the law enforcement officer's behalf) may apply for the issue of a computer access warrant if:

(a)  a recovery order is in force; and

(b)  the law enforcement officer suspects on reasonable grounds that access to data held in a computer (the target computer ) may assist in the location and safe recovery of the child to whom the recovery order relates.

Warrants sought for international assistance investigations

(4)  A law enforcement officer (or a person on the officer's behalf) may apply for the issue of a computer access warrant if the officer:

(a)  is authorised to do so under an international assistance authorisation; and

(b)  suspects on reasonable grounds that access to data held in a computer (the target computer ) is necessary, in the course of the investigation or investigative proceeding to which the authorisation relates, for the purpose of enabling evidence to be obtained of:

(i)  the commission of an offence to which the authorisation relates; or

(ii)  the identity or location of the persons suspected of committing the offence.

Warrants sought for integrity operations

(5)  A federal law enforcement officer (or another person on the federal law enforcement officer's behalf) may apply for the issue of a computer access warrant if:

(a)  an integrity authority is in effect authorising an integrity operation in relation to an offence that it is suspected has been, is being or is likely to be committed by a staff member of a target agency; and

(b)  the federal law enforcement officer suspects on reasonable grounds that access to data held in a computer (the target computer ) will assist the conduct of the integrity operation by enabling evidence to be obtained relating to the integrity, location or identity of any staff member of the target agency.

Control order access warrants

(6)  A law enforcement officer (or another person on the law enforcement officer's behalf) may apply for the issue of a computer access warrant if:

(a)  a control order is in force in relation to a person; and

(b)  the law enforcement officer suspects on reasonable grounds that access to data held in a computer (the target computer ) to obtain information relating to the person would be likely to substantially assist in:

(i)  protecting the public from a terrorist act; or

(ii)  preventing the provision of support for, or the facilitation of, a terrorist act; or

(iii)  preventing the provision of support for, or the facilitation of, the engagement in a hostile activity in a foreign country; or

(iv)  determining whether the control order, or any succeeding control order, has been, or is being, complied with.

Note:          For control orders that have been made but not come into force, see section 6C.

Procedure for making applications

(7)  An application under subsection (1), (3), (4), (5) or (6) may be made to an eligible Judge or to a nominated AAT member.

(8)  An application:

(a)  must specify:

(i)  the name of the applicant; and

(ii)  the nature and duration of the warrant sought; and

(b)  subject to this section, must be supported by an affidavit setting out the grounds on which the warrant is sought.

Unsworn applications--warrants sought for offence investigations

(9)  If a law enforcement officer believes that:

(a)  immediate access to data held in the target computer referred to in subsection (1) is necessary as described in paragraph (1)(c); and

(b)  it is impracticable for an affidavit to be prepared or sworn before an application for a warrant is made;

an application for a warrant under subsection (1) may be made before an affidavit is prepared or sworn.

(10)  If subsection (9) applies, the applicant must:

(a)  provide as much information as the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member considers is reasonably practicable in the circumstances; and

(b)  not later than 72 hours after the making of the application, send a duly sworn affidavit to the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member, whether or not a warrant has been issued.

Unsworn applications--warrants sought for recovery orders

(11)  If a law enforcement officer believes that:

(a)  immediate access to data held in the target computer referred to in subsection (3) may assist as described in paragraph (3)(b); and

(b)  it is impracticable for an affidavit to be prepared or sworn before an application for a warrant is made;

an application for a warrant under subsection (3) may be made before an affidavit is prepared or sworn.

(12)  If subsection (11) applies, the applicant must:

(a)  provide as much information as the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member considers is reasonably practicable in the circumstances; and

(b)  not later than 72 hours after the making of the application, send a duly sworn affidavit to the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member, whether or not a warrant has been issued.

Unsworn applications--control order access warrants

(13)  If a law enforcement officer believes that:

(a)  immediate access to data held in the target computer referred to in subsection (6) would be likely to substantially assist as described in paragraph (6)(b); and

(b)  it is impracticable for an affidavit to be prepared or sworn before an application for a warrant is made;

an application for a warrant under subsection (6) may be made before an affidavit is prepared or sworn.

(14)  If subsection (13) applies, the applicant must:

(a)  provide as much information as the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member considers is reasonably practicable in the circumstances; and

(b)  not later than 72 hours after the making of the application, send a duly sworn affidavit to the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member, whether or not a warrant has been issued.

Target computer

(15)  The target computer referred to in subsection (1), (3), (4), (5) or (6) may be any one or more of the following:

(a)  a particular computer;

(b)  a computer on particular premises;

(c)  a computer associated with, used by or likely to be used by, a person (whose identity may or may not be known).

Refer to Section 28 (1A) and (1B) in regards to emergency authorisations.

sections.h:
There are a number of situations that may occur where a surveillance device warrant may, or must, be revoked.

A surveillance device warrant may, by instrument in writing, be revoked by an eligible Judge or nominated AAT member on his or her own initiative at any time before the expiration of the period of validity specified in the warrant. The eligible Judge or nominated AAT member must give a copy of the instrument of revocation to the Commissioner of Police, who in turn will forward the copy of the instrument of revocation to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit, who will cause the copy of the instrument of revocation to be filed in the relevant Codafile and ensure the necessary updates in the General Register are made.

Pursuant to section 21(5) of the Act, if the investigating officer to whom a warrant is issued believes that the use of a surveillance device under the warrant is no longer necessary for the purpose of enabling evidence to be obtained of the commission of the relevant offence, or the identity or location of the offender, (or if in relation to a recovery order, for the purpose of enabling the location and safe recovery of the child to whom the order relates) the investigating officer must immediately inform the Commissioner of Police (via the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit) of that belief.

If the Commissioner of Police is satisfied that the use of a surveillance device under the warrant sought is no longer necessary for the purpose of enabling evidence to be obtained of the commission of the relevant offence, or the identity or location of the offender, (or if in relation to a recovery order, for the purpose of enabling the location and safe recovery of the child to whom the order relates) the Commissioner of Police must:

by instrument in writing, revoke the warrant; and

take the steps necessary to ensure that use of the surveillance device authorised by the warrant is discontinued.

This instrument of revocation is to be forwarded to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit who will cause the instrument of revocation to be filed in the relevant Codafile and ensure the necessary updates in the General Register are made.

sections.h:
On issue of a Commonwealth SDA warrant investigators will be advised about revocation requirements. This includes the requirement for law enforcement officers to notify the chief officer immediately upon forming the belief that use of a surveillance device under the warrant is no longer necessary for its original purpose.

SOAU will provide a prompt to investigators each month after the issue of a Commonwealth SDA to ascertain if the warrant is still required.

If the warrant is no longer required SOAU will seek a revocation in writing from the Commissioner of Police as soon as practicable on notification that the warrant is no longer required and in any event with 2 weeks of that notification.

sections.t: Records of the correspondence, including the prompts will be kept on file.

sections.h:
Upon receipt of an approved tasking request for a warrant, an emergency authorisation or a tracking device authorisation, the responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer will cause any documentation to be saved electronically on the Special Operations Application Unit secure network drive in the appropriate folder. Any further documentation concerning the warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation that is created during the application process, including supporting affidavits, is also to be saved in the appropriate folder.

Requests for access to the Special Operations Application Unit secure network drive are to be considered by the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit.

sections.h:
All hard copy applications, affidavits, warrants, emergency authorisations, tracking device authorisations and associated documentation will be stored at the Special Operations Application Unit. Once the warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation application is approved by the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member, or authorising officer, the Special Operations Application Unit officer will convey the hard copy applications, affidavit, signed warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation and associated documentation to the Warrant Administration section of the Special Operations Application Unit for storage, reporting and record keeping.

All hard copy documents will be stored in a restricted-access secure area at the Special Operations Application Unit in a Codafile specific to that warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation. Requests for access to the Special Operations Application Unit restricted-access secure area are to be considered by the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit.

sections.h:
The responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer is to cause a Codafile to be created for each warrant, emergency authorisation and tracking device authorisation that is applied for by Western Australia Police Force. Codafile creation materials are to be located in the stationery area of Special Operations Application Unit and can be re-ordered through the administration officer of Special Operations Application Unit.

The Codafile is to be appropriately labelled with the designator of the warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation and will include a print-out of the cover sheet document on red card immediately inside.

Any person updating documentation within the Codafile is to ensure the checklist component of the cover sheet document is updated accordingly.

sections.t: Documents Required to Be Kept in Codafile
sections.h:
The responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer is to cause the following documents to be kept in the relevant Codafile for each warrant, emergency authorisation and tracking device authorisation applied for by WAPOL under the Act:

Warrants:

each warrant issued;

each instrument of revocation, if applicable;

a copy of each application made for a warrant, or for an extension or variation of a warrant;

a copy of the completed Investigator Report document;

a copy of any relevant Communication of Protected Information forms;

a copy of any relevant Destruction Advice Reports; and

a copy of any evidentiary certificates issued pursuant to section 62 of the Act.

Emergency authorisations:

each written record made by the authorising officer after giving an emergency authorisation, pursuant to section 31 of the Act;

each written application for an emergency authorisation;

a copy of each application made pursuant to section 33 of the Act, in relation to approval of an emergency authorisation issued by an authorising officer;

a copy of the completed Investigator Report document;

a copy of any relevant Communication of Protected Information forms;

a copy of any relevant Destruction Advice Reports; and

a copy of any evidentiary certificates issued pursuant to section 62 of the Act.

Tracking device authorisations:

each written record made by the authorising officer after giving a tracking device authorisation, pursuant to section 40 of the Act;

each written application for a tracking device authorisation;

a copy of the completed Investigator Report document;

a copy of any relevant Communication of Protected Information forms;

a copy of any relevant Destruction Advice Reports; and

a copy of any evidentiary certificates issued pursuant to section 62 of the Act.

sections.h:
The responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer is to maintain a General Register of warrants, emergency authorisations and tracking device authorisations applied for by Western Australia Police Force, pursuant to section 53 of the Act.

Register of warrants, emergency authorisations and tracking device authorisations

(1)  The chief officer of a law enforcement agency must cause a register of warrants, emergency authorisations and tracking device authorisations sought by law enforcement officers of that agency to be kept.

(2)  The register is to specify, for each warrant sought by or on behalf of a law enforcement officer of the agency:

(a)  the date the warrant was issued or refused; and

(b)  the name of the eligible Judge or nominated AAT member who issued or refused to issue the warrant; and

(c)  if the warrant was issued:

(i)  the name of the law enforcement officer named in the warrant as the person primarily responsible for executing it; and

(ii)  if the warrant was issued in relation to a relevant offence--the relevant offence in relation to which the warrant was issued; and

(iii)  if the warrant was issued in relation to a recovery order--the date of issue of the recovery order and the name of the child to whom the order related; and

(iiia)  if the warrant was issued in relation to an international assistance authorisation--each offence to which the authorisation relates; and

(iiib)  if the warrant was issued for the purposes of an integrity operation--details identifying the integrity authority for the operation and the relevant offence in respect of which the integrity authority was granted; and

(iiic)  if the warrant is a control order warrant that was issued on the basis of a control order--the date the control order was made; and

(iiid)  if the warrant is a control order access warrant that was issued on the basis of a control order--the date the control order was made; and

(iv)  the period during which the warrant is in force; and

(v)  details of any variation or extension of the warrant.

(3)  The register is to specify, for each emergency authorisation sought by a law enforcement officer of the agency:

(a)  the date the emergency authorisation was given or refused; and

(b)  the name of the appropriate authorising officer who gave or refused to give the emergency authorisation; and

(c)  if the emergency authorisation was given:

(i)  the name of the law enforcement officer to whom the authorisation was given; and

(ii)  if the authorisation related to a relevant offence--the relevant offence in relation to which it was given; and

(iii)  if the authorisation related to a recovery order--the date of issue of the recovery order and the name of the child to whom the order related; and

(iv)  the date on which the application for approval of powers exercised under the authorisation was made; and

(v)  whether that application for approval of powers exercised under the authorisation was successful or not.

(4)  The register is to specify, for each tracking device authorisation sought by a law enforcement officer of the agency:

(a)  the date the tracking device authorisation was given or refused; and

(b)  the name of the appropriate authorising officer who gave or refused to give the tracking device authorisation; and

(c)  if the tracking device authorisation was given:

(i)  the name of the law enforcement officer to whom the authorisation was given; and

(ii)  if the authorisation related to a relevant offence--the relevant offence in relation to which it was given; and

(iii)  if the authorisation related to a recovery order--the date of issue of the recovery order and the name of the child to whom the order related; and

(iv)  if the authorisation was given for the purposes of an integrity operation--details identifying the integrity authority authorising the operation and the relevant offence in respect of which the integrity authority was granted.

(5)  The register is not a legislative instrument.

sections.h:
Upon a warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation ceasing to be in force, either through expiration or revocation, the responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer is to forward the investigating officer a copy of the Investigator Report document. This report will form the basis for the Commissioner of Police’s report to the Minister, pursuant to section 49 of the Act.

The investigator is to complete this document within two weeks of receiving it by entering the required details and return it via email to Special Operations Application Unit. The report is to be filed by the responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer in the relevant Codafile for the warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation.

sections.h:
Upon a warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation ceasing to be in force, either through expiration or revocation, the Commissioner of Police is required to, as soon as practicable, make a report to the Minister pursuant to section 49 of the Act.

Utilising the completed Investigator Report, the responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer will prepare the relevant documentation in order for the Commissioner of Police to make his or her report. This will be forwarded to the office of the Commissioner of Police through the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit.

The prepared documentation is to include:

a draft cover letter for consideration by the Commissioner of Police;

a copy of the relevant warrants, emergency authorisations or tracking device authorisations;

a copy of any instrument revoking, extending or varying the relevant warrants, emergency authorisations or tracking device authorisations; and a copy of the completed Investigator Report document for the relevant warrants, emergency authorisations or tracking device authorisations.

Special Operations Application Unit is required to keep a copy of this report. The office of the Commissioner of Police will arrange for a final copy of the report sent from the Commissioner of Police to the Minister to be provided to Special Operations Application Unit for this purpose.

sections.h:
The Commissioner of Police is required to, as soon as practicable after the end of each financial year, and in any event within 3 months after the end of the financial year, submit a report to the Minister in respect of the use of the Act by Western Australia Police Force during that financial year. On behalf of the Commissioner of Police, the responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer will prepare the relevant documentation required and forward to the office of the Commissioner of Police through the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit. This will require the responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer to research all the necessary information systems and liaise with investigators concerned in order to obtain the required data.

The prepared documentation is to include:

a draft cover letter for consideration by the Commissioner of Police;

a report that includes the following information in respect of the financial year being reported on:

the number of applications for warrants made by or on behalf of, and the number of warrants issued to, officers of Western Australia Police Force;

the number of mutual assistance applications made by or on behalf of, and the number of warrants issued as a result of such applications to, officers of Western Australia Police Force;

the number of applications for emergency authorisations made by, and the number of emergency authorisations given to, officers of Western Australia Police Force;

the number of applications for tracking device authorisations made by, and the number of such authorisations given to, officers of Western Australia Police Force;

the number of remote applications for warrants made by or on behalf of officers of Western Australia Police Force;

the number of applications for warrants, emergency authorisations and tracking device authorisations made by or on behalf of officers of Western Australia Police Force that were refused, and the reasons for refusal;

the number of mutual assistance applications made by or on behalf of officers of Western Australian Police that were refused, and the reasons for refusal;

the number of applications for extensions of warrants made by or on behalf of officers of Western Australia Police Force, the number of extensions granted or refused and the reasons why they were granted or refused;

the number of arrests made by officers of Western Australian Police on the basis (wholly or partly) of information obtained by the use of a surveillance device under a warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation;

the number of instances during that year in which the location and safe recovery of children to whom recovery orders related was assisted (wholly or partly) by information obtained by the use of a surveillance device under a warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation;

the number of prosecutions for relevant offences that were commenced during that year in which information obtained by the use of a surveillance device under a warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation was given in evidence and the number of those prosecutions in which a person was found guilty (this information can be obtained from the BriefCase case preparation, brief preparation and brief tracking system);

for each offence against a law of a foreign country in respect of which a warrant was issued as a result of a mutual assistance application made by or on behalf of law enforcement officers of the agency during the year, the offence, under a law of the Commonwealth, or of a State or a Territory, that is of the same nature as, or a substantially similar nature to, the foreign offence; and any other information relating to the use of surveillance devices and the administration of this Act that the Minister considers appropriate.

The information is to be presented in such a way as to identify the number of warrants issued, emergency authorisations given and tracking device authorisations given in respect of each different kind of surveillance device.

sections.h:
Protected information refers to:

any information obtained from the use of a surveillance device under a warrant, an emergency authorisation or a tracking device authorisation;

any information relating to an application for, the issue of, the existence of, or the expiration of, a warrant, an emergency authorisation or a tracking device authorisation;

any information relating to an application for approval of powers exercised under an emergency authorisation;

any information that is likely to enable the identification of a person, object or premises specified in a warrant, an emergency authorisation or a tracking device authorisation; and any information obtained by an officer of Western Australian Police without the authority of a warrant, a tracking device authorisation or an emergency authorisation that was subsequently approved, in contravention of the requirement for such a warrant, tracking device authorisation or emergency authorisation.

sections.h:
Any use, recording, communication or publication of protected information must be in compliance with the Act. Protected information may be used, recorded, communicated or published, or may be admitted in evidence, if it is necessary to do so for any of the following purposes:

the investigation of a relevant offence (as defined by the Act);

the making of a decision whether or not to bring a prosecution for a relevant offence;

a relevant proceeding;

an investigation of a complaint against, or into the conduct of, a public officer (as defined by the Act); and inspection, compliance and reporting purposes required under the Act.

The above list is not exhaustive and investigators and other officers concerned should direct any enquiries to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit.

The above list is not exhaustive and investigators and other officers concerned should direct any enquiries to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit.

sections.t: Communicating Protected Information to Other Law Enforcement Agencies
sections.h:
The Act contains provisions for the communication of protected information obtained through the use of surveillance devices with other law enforcement agencies. Details of this communication must be entered onto a register by Special Operations Application Unit, which is subject to inspection by oversight bodies. Therefore it is the policy of Special Operations Application Unit not to permit communication of protected information obtained through the use of surveillance devices with other law enforcement agencies without the permission of the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit.

Investigating officers and other officers concerned who wish to communicate protected information to any officer of a law enforcement agency other than Western Australia Police Force are required to complete and submit a Communication of Protected Information form to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit for consideration. This Communication of Protected Information form is to filed in the relevant Codafile, once granted or refused by the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit.

sections.t: Record Keeping of the Use of Protected Information
sections.h:
Pursuant to section 52(1)(e) to (h) of the Act, the Commissioner of Police is required to record the following details concerning the use of protected information:

details of each use by Western Australia Police Force, or by an officer of Western Australia Police Force, of information obtained by the use of a surveillance device by an officer of Western Australia Police Force;

details of each communication by an officer of Western Australia Police Force to a person other than an officer of Western Australia Police Force of information obtained by the use of a surveillance device by an officer of Western Australia Police Force;

details of each occasion when information obtained by the use of a surveillance device by an officer of Western Australia Police Force was given in evidence in a relevant proceeding; and details of each occasion when information obtained by the use of a surveillance device by an officer of Western Australia Police Force was used in the location and safe recovery of a child to whom a recovery order related.
sections.b: Pursuant to section 52(1)(e) to (h) of the Act, the Commissioner of Police is required to record the following details concerning the use of protected information: details of each use by Western Australia Police Force, or by an officer of Western Australia Police Force, of information obtained by the use of a surveillance device by an officer of Western Australia Police Force; details of each communication by an officer of Western Australia Police Force to a person other than an officer of Western Australia Police Force of information obtained by the use of a surveillance device by an officer of Western Australia Police Force; details of each occasion when information obtained by the use of a surveillance device by an officer of Western Australia Police Force was given in evidence in a relevant proceeding; and details of each occasion when information obtained by the use of a surveillance device by an officer of Western Australia Police Force was used in the location and safe recovery of a child to whom a recovery order related.

sections.h:
All access to electronic documentation that relates to protected information is only to be made available to those people entitled to deal with the protected information. Access to the protected information must be in accordance with Western Australia Police Force policy LO-01.06 Restricted Access to Information on the Police Computer System, in addition to being in compliance with Part 6 Division 1 of the Act, concerning restrictions on use, communication and publication of information.

All access to hard copy documentation that relates to protected information is only to be made available to those people entitled to deal with the protected information. Most hard copy records in relation to protected information will be stored in the secure environment of Special Operations Application Unit however investigators and other officers concerned must ensure any hard copy documentation that relates to protected information, regardless of its location, is stored in a secure-access area and access is restricted to those people entitled to deal with the protected information.

sections.t: Destruction and Retention of Protected Information
sections.h:
Any record or report that comprises protected information is to be subject to a destruction regime pursuant to section 46(1)(b) of the Act. The Commissioner of Police must cause to be destroyed any record or report that comprises protected information:

as soon as practicable after the making of the record or report, if the Commissioner of Police is satisfied that no civil or criminal proceeding to which the material contained in the record or report relates has been, or is likely to be, commenced, and that the material contained in the record or report is not likely to be required in connection with any activity or purpose referred to in sections 45(4), 45(5) or 45A(1) of the Act; and within the period of 5 years after the making of the record or report, and within each period of 5 years thereafter, unless, before the end of that period, the Commissioner of Police is satisfied in relation to the material contained in the record or report of a matter referred to in the paragraph above and certifies to that effect [ie. that the matter is ongoing].

Those records or reports that are consistent with the Western Australia Police Force Serious Crime Exhibit Retention Management policy (CR-15.2 Serious Crime Exhibit Retention and Management) will be dealt with under those provisions. This is to ensure Western Australia Police Force remains compliant under the Act as well as consistent with the objective of the policy, being to ensure all serious crime exhibits are managed in such a way as to protect any future loss of evidence, even in circumstances where it is thought a trial (guilty verdict) has brought a conclusion to a crime.

The responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer will maintain a destructions register concerning all records and reports comprising protected information and will continually reconcile the storage of records and reports comprising protected information against the requirement for destruction. The destructions register is to include the following information in relation to the relevant warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation:

the warrant or authorisation designator;

the date of application;

whether the application was granted, refused or withdrawn;

the period in force;

the date of revocation or expiration;

the name of the investigating officer;

the unit the investigating officer is attached to;

the destruction review date (to be adjusted as required);

whether or not a Destruction Advice Report has been received;

details of the use of protected information;

whether the records and reports are to be destroyed or retained;

whether the records and reports are subject to the Criminal Organisation Control Act 2012 (WA);

whether the records and reports are subject to the Western Australia Police Force Serious Crime Exhibit Retention Management policy;

whether the records and reports have been inspected by the Commonwealth Ombudsman;

whether destruction of the records and reports has been authorised by the Commissioner of Police;

the location of all records and reports; and

whether the records and reports have been destroyed.

The investigating officer is required to complete and submit a Destruction Advice Report concerning the above information to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit with a recommendation as to whether the records and reports are to be destroyed or retained.

Once the decision to destroy records and reports comprising protected information has been approved, the destruction of said records and reports is to occur in accordance with Western Australia Police Force policy RM 10 Records Retention and Disposal Guidelines and in consultation with the Records Management Centre, Office of Information Management.

SOAU has a dedicated destructions officer to ensure no destructions are carried out in contravention of section 46 of the Act. Any information or records required for destruction by the investigator are now forwarded to SOAU to facilitate.

All investigators are now provided with a copy of these Standard Operating Procedures which refers to the destruction process and retention of records for longer than 5 years.

sections.b: Any record or report that comprises protected information is to be subject to a destruction regime pursuant to section 46(1)(b) of the Act. The Commissioner of Police must cause to be destroyed any record or report that comprises protected information: as soon as practicable after the making of the record or report, if the Commissioner of Police is satisfied that no civil or criminal proceeding to which the material contained in the record or report relates has been, or is likely to be, commenced, and that the material contained in the record or report is not likely to be required in connection with any activity or purpose referred to in sections 45(4), 45(5) or 45A(1) of the Act; and within the period of 5 years after the making of the record or report, and within each period of 5 years thereafter, unless, before the end of that period, the Commissioner of Police is satisfied in relation to the material contained in the record or report of a matter referred to in the paragraph above and certifies to that effect [ie. that the matter is ongoing]. Those records or reports that are consistent with the Western Australia Police Force Serious Crime Exhibit Retention Management policy (CR-15.2 Serious Crime Exhibit Retention and Management) will be dealt with under those provisions. This is to ensure Western Australia Police Force remains compliant under the Act as well as consistent with the objective of the policy, being to ensure all serious crime exhibits are managed in such a way as to protect any future loss of evidence, even in circumstances where it is thought a trial (guilty verdict) has brought a conclusion to a crime. The responsible Special Operations Application Unit officer will maintain a destructions register concerning all records and reports comprising protected information and will continually reconcile the storage of records and reports comprising protected information against the requirement for destruction. The destructions register is to include the following information in relation to the relevant warrant, emergency authorisation or tracking device authorisation: the warrant or authorisation designator; the date of application; whether the application was granted, refused or withdrawn; the period in force; the date of revocation or expiration; the name of the investigating officer; the unit the investigating officer is attached to; the destruction review date (to be adjusted as required); whether or not a Destruction Advice Report has been received; details of the use of protected information; whether the records and reports are to be destroyed or retained; whether the records and reports are subject to the Criminal Organisation Control Act 2012 (WA); whether the records and reports are subject to the Western Australia Police Force Serious Crime Exhibit Retention Management policy; whether the records and reports have been inspected by the Commonwealth Ombudsman; whether destruction of the records and reports has been authorised by the Commissioner of Police; the location of all records and reports; and whether the records and reports have been destroyed. The investigating officer is required to complete and submit a Destruction Advice Report concerning the above information to the Officer-in-Charge of Special Operations Application Unit with a recommendation as to whether the records and reports are to be destroyed or retained. Once the decision to destroy records and reports comprising protected information has been approved, the destruction of said records and reports is to occur in accordance with Western Australia Police Force policy RM 10 Records Retention and Disposal Guidelines and in consultation with the Records Management Centre, Office of Information Management. SOAU has a dedicated destructions officer to ensure no destructions are carried out in contravention of section 46 of the Act. Any information or records required for destruction by the investigator are now forwarded to SOAU to facilitate. All investigators are now provided with a copy of these Standard Operating Procedures which refers to the destruction process and retention of records for longer than 5 years. Please see attached forms: SDA Cth FORM Destruction Advice Report SDA Cth FORM Communication of Protected Information ANNEXURES Investigator Report Example

sections.h:
Commonwealth SDA (Warrant approved/issued)

Your application for a Commonwealth SDA warrant(s)/or extension to warrant XX of 202X, has gone to court and a warrant(s) was issued/or extension granted by [TITLE – Justice] XXXXX for a period of XX days.

Please note the EXPIRY DATE of XX/XX/202X.

It is your responsibility to submit a supplementary TRF to the State Intelligence and command Portfolio, Covert Coordinator 14 days prior to the expiry date if you believe that you are going to require an extension/further extension.

Please liaise with the Police Technical Unit, Operations Manager on 08 9315 4409 (all hours) to arrange the installation or retrieval of surveillance devices pursuant to your warrant (s).

As the applicant for a Commonwealth SDA you must be aware and abide by the following legislation compliance matters:

For assistance on Commonwealth SDA procedures seek further information Blue Book / State Intelligence and Command / Intelligence Services / Special Operations Application Unit / Commonwealth SDA Procedures Execution of Warrant:

Should you as the applicant cease for any reason to have responsibility for the execution of the warrant, the Chief Officer of the agency in writing, must nominate another person as the law enforcement officer. If this is unable to be done, the warrant should be revoked and another warrant sought.

(e.g. transfer to another business unit, extended leave etc.) See s6(3) Surveillance Devices Act 2004 (Commonwealth)

Storage of Protected Information:

Section 46(1)(a) of the Act requires the chief officer to ensure that every record comprising protected information is kept in a secure place that is not accessible to people who are not entitled to deal with the information. The definition of 'protected information' under s 44(1) of the Act includes information obtained from the use of a surveillance device.

PLEASE ENSURE THAT ANY STORAGE OF PRODUCT ON USB’s, HARD DRIVES OR DISCS ARE ENCRYPTED AND ONLY AVAILABLE TO THOSE PERSON ENTITLED TO DEAL WITH IT.

Keeping Records of Each use:

Under s 45 of the Act, the use, recording, communication, or publication of protected information is prohibited unless it is for a permitted purpose detailed under s 45(4) of the Act. Section 45(5) of the Act further provides permitted purposes to use, record, communicate or publish, or admit in evidence protected information.

Under s 52(1)(e) of the Act, the chief officer of a law enforcement agency must keep 'details of each use' by the agency, or by a law enforcement officer of the agency, of information obtained using a surveillance device or access to data held in a computer. Section 52(1)(f) of the Act requires the same for ‘details of each communication' by a law enforcement officer of the agency to a person other than a law enforcement officer of the agency.

PLEASE ENSURE RECORDS ARE KEPT ON EACH USE AND THE RECORD KEEPING REQUIREMENTS SURROUNDING EACH USE. PLEASE KEEP DETAILED RUNNING SHEETS ON EACH USE OF THE SDA AND SUPPLY WITH THE COMPLETE UCL WITH A COPY OF THESE RUNNING SHEETS TO BE KEPT ON FILE FOR INSPECTION BY THE COMMONWEALTH OMBUDSMAN.

Revocation of Warrants:

The chief officer must revoke a surveillance device warrant, by instrument in writing, if the prescribed circumstances in s 21 of the Act apply. This includes where the chief officer is satisfied that the use of a surveillance device under the warrant is no longer necessary for the purpose of enabling evidence to be obtained of the commission of a relevant offence or the identity or location of an offender.

PLEASE NOTIFY SOAU IMMEDIATELY IF THE WAARANT IS NO LONGER NECESSARY FOR ITS ORIGINAL PURPOSE. SOAU WILL THEN ARRANGE REVOCATION WITH THE CHIEF OFFICER.

Final Investigators Report:

Section 49(1) of the Act states that the chief officer must as soon as practicable after a warrant or authority ceases to be in force, make a report to the Minister.

PLEASE REFERENCE RECORDS KEPT FOR THE USE OF THE SDA INCLUDING ANY RUNNING SHEETS WHEN COMPILING THE INVESTIGATOR REPORT.

For assistance in completing this report please refer to example report situated at Blue Book / State Intelligence and Command / Intelligence Services / Special Operations Application Unit / Commonwealth SDA Procedures NOTE - If you are making an application for any covert services, either for a telecommunication intercept, surveillance devices, physical surveillance or undercover officers you are required to complete a 'WAPOL Tasking Request Form’ Please contact the SOAU Covert Applications Supervisor on 9223522 if you have any queries or concerns.

Pursuant to section 46(1)(b) of the Surveillance Devices Act 2004 (Cth) (the Act) any record or report (including optical discs, electronic product and related material) that comprises protected information, as defined in the Act, is to be subject to a destruction regime. The Investigating Officer is to complete the Destruction Advice Report below and submit it to Special Operations Application Unit in order to facilitate the destruction process.

This form is to be completed by any Investigating Officer who wishes to communicate protected information, as defined by section 45 of the Surveillance Devices Act 2004 (Cth) (the Act), to any officer of a law enforcement agency other than Western Australia Police.

It is the policy of Special Operations Application Unit (SOAU) not to permit communication of protected information obtained through the use of surveillance devices with other law enforcement agencies without the permission of the Officer-in-Charge of SOAU. The use, communication and publication of protected information is restricted under the Act and is subject to record keeping requirements if communication of the protected information is approved by the OIC SOAU.

The Investigating Officer is to complete the below form and submit to SOAU for approval.

(Check if all devices have been removed.)

sections.h:
Keeping Records of Each use:

Under s 45 of the Act, the use, recording, communication, or publication of protected information is prohibited unless it is for a permitted purpose detailed under s 45(4) of the Act. Section 45(5) of the Act further provides permitted purposes to use, record, communicate or publish, or admit in evidence protected information.

Under s 52(1)(e) of the Act, the chief officer of a law enforcement agency must keep 'details of each use' by the agency, or by a law enforcement officer of the agency, of information obtained using a surveillance device or access to data held in a computer. Section 52(1)(f) of the Act requires the same for ‘details of each communication' by a law enforcement officer of the agency to a person other than a law enforcement officer of the agency.

sections.h:
Section 45(7) of the Surveillance Devices Act 2004 (Cth) If protected information obtained through the use of a surveillance device by a law enforcement officer of a particular law enforcement agency (the originating agency):

is communicated to another law enforcement agency (by communicating it to the chief officer or another officer of that agency); or is communicated to any agency that is not a law enforcement agency (other than the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation and the agencies within the meaning of the Intelligence Services Act 2001) (by communicating it to the officer in charge of that agency or to another officer of that agency);

for a particular purpose, the protected information that has been so communicated:

may be communicated from one officer to another within that agency or organisation for that purpose only; and must not, except for the purpose of bringing a relevant proceeding, or a State or Territory relevant proceeding, be communicated to any person who is not a member of that agency or organisation.
